title: Hashes, MACs, and signatures differ
description: A hash gives integrity only, a MAC adds authenticity with a shared key, and a signature adds non-repudiable authenticity with a public key.
body: Use when choosing how to protect data from tampering or prove its origin. A cryptographic hash (SHA-256) detects accidental change but anyone can recompute it, so alone it does not prove authenticity; an HMAC adds a shared secret so only key holders can produce or verify the tag; a digital signature (Ed25519/ECDSA) lets anyone verify with a public key while only the private key can sign, giving non-repudiation. Do not protect a downloaded artifact with a bare hash an attacker could replace alongside it, and avoid using a hash where authenticity is required. Actions: pick hash for integrity, HMAC for shared-key authenticity, signature for public verification. Validation signals include verification failing on tamper. Risk boundary: a bare hash for authenticity is forgeable; verify the goal matches the primitive.
